coredata.dc:title: Chitosan-carbon quantum dots as a new platform for highly sensitive insulin impedimetric aptasensor
coredata.dc:creator: Abazar, Farbod
Noorbakhsh, Abdollah
coredata.dc:description: Considering the importance of measuring insulin in the prevention and diagnosis of diabetes-related illnesses, here we introduced an ultrasensitive electrochemical aptamer-based sensor for detection of insulin in human blood serum. In this research, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using simple, fast and cheap methods from candle soot. By dispersion of the CQDs in chitosan (Chi) matrix, homogenous CQD-Chit nano-composite was obtained. The experimental results demonstrate the good advantages of proposed CQD-Chit nano-composite, such as homogeneity, high stability and multiple amino groups for covalent immobilization of aptamer and fabrication of highly selective insulin aptasensor. CQD-Chit nano-composite was characterized by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDAX), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and electrochemical techniques. Under the optimized conditions with electrochemical impedance spectroscopy technique, a linear response ranges from 0.5 nM to 10 nM with the sensitivity of 80.07 Ω/nM and the very low detection limit of 106.8 pM was obtained for insulin detection. The fabricated aptasensor is validated in comparison with standard methods for detection of insulin in human blood serum. Also, the analytical performances of the fabricated aptasensor can be a superiority compared to the current insulin electrochemical biosensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Insulin detection
Impedimetric aptasensor
Modifies electrode
Biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs and CQD-Chit composite
selectivity and real sample analysis of aptasensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was financially supported by University of Isfahan . The authors thank Dr. Ensiyeh Sharifi for her valuable discussion. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of as-synthesized CQDs, inset shows histogram of particle diameter size. (B) Nyquist plots of the (a) GC, (b) GC/CQD and (c) GC/CQD-Chit electrode recorded in 0.3 M KCl electrolyte solution containing 10 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− at an applied potential of 0.22 V and a frequency range of 0.1–10 kHz. Dot and dash lines represent the experimental and the fitted data respectively, based on modified Randle's model (Inset of the Fig).
FESM images of (A) CQD-Chit composite, (B) as (A) in higher magnification and (C) CQDs.

(A) Schematic diagram of the fabrication and operation of the aptasensor. (B) EIS complex plane plots obtained on (a) GC, (b) GC/CQD-Chit, (c) GC/CQD-Chit/GLA, (d) GC/CQD-Chit/GLA/APT and (e) GC/CQD-Chit/GLA/APT/BSA electrodes in 0.1 M KCl electrolyte solution containing 10 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4−. EDAX spectra of (C) CQD-Chit/GLA film and (D) CQD-Chit/GLA/APT, (The Al peaks refers to the aluminum sub-layer).

(A) Nyquist plots of aptasensor in various concentration of insulin, (B) Calibration curve for the aptasensor in the probe solution in the presence of the different concentration of insulin: 0.5, 1.0, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 250, 500 nmol L−1. Inset shows the resulting linear range.

(A) Selectivity of the aptasensor for insulin detection compared with other biological interferences, Insulin used at 100 nM and other tested molecules were all used at 100 μM. (B) Aptasensor response values to 5 nM insulin for 5 electrode samples made in the same conditions.
Comparison for the performance characteristic of insulin aptasensor in this work with other modified electrodes reported.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a Indium tin oxide, b Silver nano flowers, c Reduced grapheme oxide, d Screen printed electrode, e Mulit-wall carbon nanotubes, f Glassy carbon electrode, g Carbon nanotube, h Carbon ceramic electrode, i Carbon paste electrode, j Pencil graphite electrode, kElectrochemical impedance spectroscopy, lAmperometry, mCyclic voltammetry, n Differential pulse voltammetry, o Flow-injection amperometry.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Insulin determination in human serum samples by the fabricated aptasensor and immunoassay method.

head.ce:title: Chitosan-carbon quantum dots as a new platform for highly sensitive insulin impedimetric aptasensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanotechnology Engineering, Faculty of Advanced Sciences and Technologies, University of Isfahan, Isfahan, 81746-73441, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Advanced Sciences and Technologies

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanotechnology Engineering, Faculty of Advanced Sciences and Technology, University of Isfahan, Isfahan, 81746-73441. Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Considering the importance of measuring insulin in the prevention and diagnosis of diabetes-related illnesses, here we introduced an ultrasensitive electrochemical aptamer-based sensor for detection of insulin in human blood serum. In this research, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using simple, fast and cheap methods from candle soot. By dispersion of the CQDs in chitosan (Chi) matrix, homogenous CQD-Chit nano-composite was obtained. The experimental results demonstrate the good advantages of proposed CQD-Chit nano-composite, such as homogeneity, high stability and multiple amino groups for covalent immobilization of aptamer and fabrication of highly selective insulin aptasensor. CQD-Chit nano-composite was characterized by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDAX), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and electrochemical techniques. Under the optimized conditions with electrochemical impedance spectroscopy technique, a linear response ranges from 0.5 nM to 10 nM with the sensitivity of 80.07 Ω/nM and the very low detection limit of 106.8 pM was obtained for insulin detection. The fabricated aptasensor is validated in comparison with standard methods for detection of insulin in human blood serum. Also, the analytical performances of the fabricated aptasensor can be a superiority compared to the current insulin electrochemical biosensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Insulin is one of the most important peptide hormones that produced in pancreatic b-cells to regulating the metabolism of glucose in the blood, consists of two polypeptide chains linked by two disulfide bridges, an A chain of 21 amino acid residues and a B chain of 30 amino acid residues. Until today, many effective methods investigate for insulin determination such as bioassays, immunoassay, and chromatographic methods, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay. However, most of these methods for the detection of insulin are slow, time-consuming and require expensive instruments [1,2]. Therefore, alternative electrochemical sensors based on modified electrodes using nanomaterials such as ruthenium metallodendrimer, silica nanoparticles, carbon nanotube (CNT), nickel powder for insulin detection were developed [3–5]. Although electrochemical sensors modified with nanomaterials have shown low detection limit, simple and fast fabrication, using nanomaterials as an effective parameter in immobilization, accumulation amount, orientation and assembly density controlling of aptamers for the optimized recognition ability can lead to the fabrication of electrochemical aptasensors with unique features compared to conventional electrochemical sensors [6].

Aptamers are single-stranded nucleic acids or peptide molecules with the ability to bind specifically and strongly to their targets by conformational change upon target binding [7]. Aptamers are usually obtained by selection from a random oligonucleotide library via a technique known as SELEX (systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment) [8]. Aptamers, due to their high binding affinity and specificity, have attracted much attention in biomedical field applications including biosensing [9], targeted drug delivery [10], gene therapy [11] and cancer cell imaging [12]. Electrochemical aptasensors (ECASs) are the new type of electrochemical sensors utilize with aptamers capable of specific binding to targets such as metal ions, organic molecules, biological cells, etc [13]. Also, excellent features of aptamers, such as chemical stability, low immunogenicity and automated synthesis with variety choices of nucleotide or phosphate functional groups make aptamers favorable choice as sensitive biological elements in the design and fabrication of high-performance electrochemical aptasensors [14]. Recently, the interaction between a 30-base-long insulin-binding aptamer and insulin is studied. The CD spectrum of this aptamer showed a four-stranded parallel G-quadruplex conformation [15]. In this study, this conformation change use as a sign of impedance change for insulin detection.

Recently, carbon-based nanomaterials have attracted considerable interest in biosensing. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are the last edge of carbon-based nanomaterials development. CQDs are known as carbon particles with small size (1–10 nm) with size-dependent optical properties [16]. CQDs have received much attention in a wide range of applications including chemical sensing [17], biosensing [18], bioimaging [19], drug delivery [20], photocatalysis [21] and electrocatalysis [22] due to specific properties of CQDs over other nanomaterials such as tunable fluorescence emissions, facile functionalization and excellent physicochemical, photochemical stability, low cost and ease of synthesis [23]. However, little research has been done on the electrochemistry of these nanoscale materials in comparison to other carbon-based nanomaterials, including graphene [24], carbon nanotubes [2], nanodiscs [25] and carbon black [26]. Therefore, here an electrochemical aptasensor based on favorable characteristic properties of CQDs and chitosan as an ideal immobilization film for sensitive and selective detection of insulin in blood serum developed for the first time.

Dispersion of nanomaterials plays a critical role in many properties of materials [27]. Nanomaterials due to the high surface area to volume ratio and high surface free energy have a grating tendency to agglomeration. Proper dispersion of nanomaterials is one of the main challenges for development and application of new nano-composites. Researches show many limitations for using nanoparticles because of their tendency to agglomeration. So, it is important to control the dispersion of nonmaterial. Various literatures show that particle size, surface chemistry, aspect ratio, agglomerate density and strength, and polymer type strongly affect the dispersion of carbon-based nanoparticles into the polymeric matrix and the electrical percolation threshold of the related nano-composites [28]. Chit is a good candidate for dispersion of nanomaterials and fabrication of nano-composites [29]. Recently this compound was used for fabrication of various carbon-based nano-composites [30]. Previously we studied the graphene-Chit and carbon nanotube-Chit nano-composites for fabrication of sensitive cancer marker biosensors [31,32].

In this work, CQDs were synthesized using very simple, fast and cost-effective methods from candle soot. Also, a very simple, fast and cost-effective dispersing of CQDs without adding any dispersing agents or chemical functionalization into the chitosan matrix was investigated. In the following, we focus on the application of novel CQD-Chit composite as a new platform for immobilization of SS-DNA molecules and the fabrication of highly sensitive electrochemical insulin aptasensor as a model. This research is the first use of CQDs-Chit nano-composite for the immobilization of aptamer molecules and fabrication of insulin impedimetric aptasensor. Several experimental parameters were optimized and investigated in the present work. The proposed aptasensor was used for selective and sensitive determination of insulin in real samples. The performance of synthesis CQDs-Chit in electrochemical biosensing was investigated. Also, the analytical performances of the proposed insulin aptasensor in comparison to other insulin electrochemical sensors and commercial insulin sensing systems were studied.
All materials and reagents, apparatus and measurements are described in detail in the supplementary file.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs and CQD-Chit composite
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure that is used here for the preparation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is based on the method reported in previous researches [33] with further modifications. Briefly, 4 mg of soot obtained from the burning candle was added into 7 ml of N,N-dimethyl formamide (DMF) and placed in an ultrasonic bath for three h. Then, the obtained solution centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min, the supernatant was collected and dried at 100 C for two h. The obtained CQDs powder dissolved in distilled water at different concentration and placed in an ultrasonic bath for three h for electrode modification. For preparation of Chi solution, 2 mg of Chit powder was dissolved in 1 ml of 2% acetic acid and placed in an ultrasonic bath for one h. The CQD-Chit composite (Fig. S1) was prepared by simple mixing the CQDs and Chitosan solutions in various ratios.

Glassy carbon electrode was cleaned by polishing with 0.05 μm alumina slurry. Then 2 μL of the CQD-Chit composite was drop cast on the GC electrode surface and dried at room temperature to obtain GC/CQD-Chit modified electrode. For the immobilization of the amino-functionalized aptamers on the electrode surface, the GC/CQD-Chit electrode was incubated in a 2% glutaraldehyde (GLA) solution for one h at room temperature to obtain GC/CQD-Chit/GLA. Subsequently, 2 μL of the aptamer solution was dropped on the GC/CQD-Chit/GLA surface and incubated for one h at room temperature to obtain GC/CQD-Chit/GLA/APT. To remove the non attached aptamer molecules the GC/CQD-Chit/GLA/APT electrode was washed with PBS and distilled water for 5 min. Then this modified electrode was incubated in 0.5 mg ml−1 of bovine serum albumin (BSA) solution for 60 min to block unreacted surface sites. Finally, the electrode was thoroughly rinsed with PBS and distilled water. This electrode named as GC/CQD-Chit/GLA/APT/BSA electrode. Each step of electrode modification was investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). For detection of insulin, the as-prepared GC/CQD-Chit/GLA/APT/BSA electrode was incubated with different concentrations of insulin for one h. Then, this electrode was rinsed with ultra-pure water and PBS to remove any un-adsorbed or weakly-adsorbed insulin from the electrode surface. The observed change in charge transfer resistance (R ct) in the absence and presence of insulin was used as an aptasensor response for determination of insulin concentration,

The TEM image and corresponding particle size histogram of synthesized CQDs are shown in Fig. 1 A. As can be seen, most of CQDs are less than 10 nm. According to the definition given for CQDs, TEM image represents the successful synthesis of CQDs. Also, this result is in very good agreement with previous works that describe the solvothermal synthesis of CQDs [34,35]. The charge transfer properties of synthesized CQDs and CQD-Chit composite were investigated by using EIS. According to Table S1, the value of charge-transfer resistance (R ct) for GC, GC/CQD and GC/CQD-Chit electrodes was estimated to be 255.74Ω, 14.05Ω and 23.13Ω, respectively (Fig. 1B). These results indicated lower R ct at the surface of GC/CQD and GC/CQD-Chit interface compared to that of the bare GC electrode, which is similar to previous reports [36]. Fig. 2 shows the SEM images of CQDs and CQD-Chit composite. As can be seen CQD-Chit composite create a homogeneous layer on the electrode surface. By comparison of SEM image of CQDs-Chit and CQDs (Fig. 2B and C), it is clear that the chit matrix obviously increases the dispersion of very fine CQDs nanoparticles. Also, the AFM images of CQDs-Chit demonstrate the very good dispersion of CQDs in Chitosam matrix (Fig. S2). On the other hand, Chit matrix has a great adherence to the electrode surface and forms the stable composite on the electrode surface [37]. The stability of CQD-Chit film on the electrode surface was examined using cyclic voltammetry. As can be shown in Fig. S3, CQD-Chit composite can form a very stable film with good stability on the electrode surface. As a result, unique properties of CQD-Chit composite such as high homogeneity, very good stability, very low charge-transfer resistance and the existence of suitable functional groups on the surface (such as amine groups) make it a perfect candidate for immobilization of aptamer molecules and fabrication of electrochemical aptasensor. FTIR spectra analysis was also performed to investigate the chemical structure and functional groups of CQDs and CQD-Chit film. Fig. S4 compares the FT-IR spectra of Chit, CQDs and CQD-Chi composite. These spectra are described in the supplementary section in detail. The typical characteristic peaks of CQDs and Chit are present in FTIR spectra of CQD-Chit composite. Also, the existence of new at 1563 in CQD-Chit film and shift in adsorption band of NH and OH groups of Chit and CQD, respectively in the CQD-Chit film confirm the strong interaction between CQD and Chit and fabrication of CQD-Chit composite.

A schematic structure of the aptasensor is shown in Fig. 3 A. Each step of electrode modification characterized using EIS method. The Nyquist plots of bare GC, GC/CQD-Chit, GC/CQD-Chit/GLA, GC/CQD-Chit/GLA/APT, and GC/CQD-Chit/GLA/APT/BSA electrodes are shown in Fig. 3B. Also, Table S2 summarizes the electrochemical parameters extracted from these Nyquist plots. The results show that the modification of bare GC electrode (curve a) with the CQD-Chit film (curve b) leads to a decrease in values of Rct . This phenomenon may be due to high conductivity and large surface area of CQD that resulted in the facilitation of the charge transfer process at the electrode interface. Also, electrostatic attraction between positively charged amino groups of the Chit and anionic probe, leading to an increase in mass transport of redox probe to the surface. In the following, Rct values obviously increased after incubation with glutaraldehyde and aptamer molecules (curve c and d, respectively). This increase could be related to the blocking effect of the glutaraldehyde and aptamer molecules and also, the repulsive force between negatively charged phosphate groups of DNA and redox probe. Furthermore, as can be expected adsorption of BSA on the surface of the GC/CQD-Chit/GLA/APT electrode caused an increase in the Rct value (curve e). The obtained results are consistent with the results of recent studies [31]. As can be shown in Fig. 3A, in the presence of insulin, the specific interaction of insulin with aptamer molecules can induce the folding of aptamer molecules on the electrode surface and enhance the blocking effect of aptamer molecules. As a result, in the presence of insulin, the Rct value can be increased dramatically. Also to investigating the immobilization of aptamer molecules on the surface of the electrode, the EDAX spectra of CQD-Chit/GLA and CQD-Chit/GLA/APT electrodes were investigated (Fig. 3C and D). The presence of phosphorus element in the EDAX spectra of the CQD-Chit/GLA/APT modified electrode indicates the successful immobilization of insulin aptamer molecules at the electrode surface theses results are in very good agreement with EIS results. The Al peaks refer to the aluminum sub-layer (supplementary file, section1.2).

To achieve the highest sensitivity of the proposed aptasensor the main experimental parameters such as CQDs to Chit ratio, aptamer concentration, aptamer incubation time, insulin incubation time and washing time of electrode surface, were investigated and optimized using EIS technique. The details were described in a supplementary file (Section 2.3, Fig. S5). Experimental results show that CQDs to Chit ratio of 3:2, the aptamer concentration of 5μM, aptamer incubation time of 60 min, washing time of 5 min and insulin incubation time of 60 min, are the best conditions.

To investigate the analytical performances of the proposed aptasensor, various concentrations of insulin in the rage of 0.5–500 nM were used, and the corresponding Nyquist plots and aptasensor response were recorded (Fig. 4 A). By increasing the insulin concentration, an obvious change in Nyquist plots (increasing the Rct value) can be observed the obtained data are shown in Table S3. Fig. 4B shows the aptasensor response versus insulin concentration. A linear increase in aptasensor response can be seen by increasing the insulin concentration over the range of 500 pM to 10 nM based on the Eq. (1)
(1) ΔRct (Ω)=80.07 [Insulin](nM) + 19.498 (Ω) R2=0.9998

At high insulin concentrations, calibration curve deviates from linearity due to the saturation of the electrode surface. From the Eq. (1) (using the calibration line method, supplementary file), the estimated limit of detection (LOD) and sensitivity for GC/CQD-Chit/GLA/APT/BSA electrode were 106.8 pM (S/N = 3) and 80.07 Ω nM−1, respectively. The proposed aptasensor showed desirable performance and impressive LOD and sensitivity in comparison with other label-free insulin aptasensor [38] and other electrochemical insulin sensors/biosensors (Table 1 ).

The reproducibility of the proposed aptasensor was examined as an important parameter. For these propose five individual GC/CQD-Chit/GLA/APT/BSA electrodes were prepared, and responses of each electrode toward 10 nM insulin were monitored and compared (Fig. 5 A). Based on these results the relative standard deviation of 4.1% was obtained. Moreover, the long-term storage stability of aptasensor was proved by investigation of the aptasesor response toward 10 nM insulin, after the aptasensor has been stored at 4 °C in 0.01 M PBS buffer for one week. After one week, the sensor retained about 96% of the initial response. These results exhibit a very good reproducibility and excellent stability of modified surface that could be attributed to the high homogeneity and stability of CQD-Chit film and efficient immobilization of aptamer molecules to the surface of the CQD-Chit film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: selectivity and real sample analysis of aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: In control experiment the adsorption of insulin molecules on the surface of CQD-Chi composite was investigated by recording the Nyquist plots of GC/CQD-Chit electrode before and after incubation with insulin. No obvious change can be observed in Nyquist plots (Fig. S6). This result demonstrates that insulin molecule can not be adsorbed on the surface of CQD-Chit composite and only insulin aptamer molecules can capture insulin molecules.

Selectivity of the proposed aptasensor was investigated in the presence of some interfering species such as bovine serum albumin (BSA), ascorbic acid, uric acid, and L-cysteine. As shown in Fig. 5B, only a considerable response was observed upon the addition of insulin, and for other species, no obvious response can be obtained. Also, the detection of insulin in real samples was studied. For this propose, human blood serum samples were diluted in PBS buffer, and four standard samples were prepared by addition of 0.0 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 nM insulin to human blood serum. The samples were analyzed using proposed aptaseor by standard addition method. As shown in Table 2 , the obtained recoveries are in the range of 96.8%–105.6%. These results represent the very good accuracy and selectivity of proposed aptasensor in the complex matrix of human blood serum. The high selectivity of the aptasensor is attributed to the effective and efficient immobilization of insulin aptamer molecules and also, very good blocking effect of BSA molecule for GC/CQD-Chit/GLA/APT/BSA electrode. In another experiment, the aptasensor response was validated by comparison the aptasensor response toward insulin with the commercial method. For this propose the prepared standard insulin samples by human blood serum, were analyzed with commercial ADVIA Centaur® CP Immunoassay System. The obtained results were compared with proposed insulin aptasensor (Table 2). As can be seen for the commercial method the obtained recoveries are in the range of 98.5–101.9%. These results proved that the proposed aptasensor is reliable and has very good accuracy for determination of insulin in human serum samples and can be used in clinical applications

In the present work, CQD-Chit nano-composite was synthesized using very simple, fast and cost-effective method. CQD-Chit nano-composite as an ideal material for modification of electrode was used for the immobilization of insulin aptamer molecules and fabrication of highly sensitive impedimetric insulin aptasenor as a model. The obtained results demonstrate that CQDs-Chit nano-composite has many advantages including high homogeneity, high stability, very low charge-transfer resistance and the existence of amine moiety for efficient immobilization of aptamer molecules on the electrode surface and fabrication of sensitive electrochemical aptasensor.

The fabricated impedimetric insulin aptasensor was used for determination of insulin concentration in standard and human blood serum samples. The experiment results indicate that the proposed aptasensor can be used for determination of insulin concentration with very high selectivity, good accuracy, low detection limit (106.8 pM) and high sensitivity in physiological pH. The analytical performances of the fabricated aptasensor, are comparable or better than other electrochemical insulin sensors have been published recently. Also the proposed aptasensor response was validated by comparison with current commercial insulin sensor. Very simple and cost-effective fabrication, good reproducibility, high stability, low detection limit, high sensitivity and selectivity and very good accuracy for determination of insulin in human blood serum, make the proposed insulin aptasensor, as one of the good candidates for clinical applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was financially supported by University of Isfahan. The authors thank Dr. Ensiyeh Sharifi for her valuable discussion.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.127281.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flow injection amperometric detection of insulin at cobalt hydroxide nanoparticles modified carbon ceramic electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Habibi, E., Omidinia, E., Heidari, H., Fazli, M., Flow injection amperometric detection of insulin at cobalt hydroxide nanoparticles modified carbon ceramic electrode, Anal. Biochem. 495 (2016) 37-41.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Insulin sensor based on nanoparticle-decorated multiwalled carbon nanotubes modified electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Martínez-Periñán, E., Revenga-Parra, M., Gennari, M., Pariente, F., Mas-Ballesté, R., Zamora, F., Lorenzo, E., Insulin sensor based on nanoparticle-decorated multiwalled carbon nanotubes modified electrode, Sens. Actuator B Chem. 222 (2016) 331-338.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrocatalytic determination of traces of insulin using a novel silica nanoparticles-Nafion modified glassy carbon electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Amini, N., Gholivand, M.B., Shamsipur, M., Electrocatalytic determination of traces of insulin using a novel silica nanoparticles-Nafion modified glassy carbon electrode, J. Electroanal. Chem. 714 (2014) 70-75.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Insulin oxidation and determination at carbon electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, M., Mullens, C., Gorski, W., Insulin oxidation and determination at carbon electrodes, Anal. Chem. 77 (2005) 6396-6401.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Picomolar detection of insulin at renewable nickel powder-doped carbon composite electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Salimi, A., Roushani, M., Soltanian, S., Hallaj, R., Picomolar detection of insulin at renewable nickel powder-doped carbon composite electrode, Anal. Chem. 79 (2007) 7431-7438.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review: aptamer-based analytical strategies using the nanomaterials for environmental and human monitoring of toxic heavy metals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Farzin, L., Shamsipur, M., Sheibani, S., A review: Aptamer-based analytical strategies using the nanomaterials for environmental and human monitoring of toxic heavy metals, Talanta 174 (2017) 619-627.
Nezlin, R., Aptamers in immunological research, Immunol. Lett. 162 (2014) 252-255.
Wu, Y.X., Kwon, Y.J., Aptamers: The “evolution” of SELEX, Methods 106 (2016) 21-28.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescent aptasensor based on a DNA pyramid nanostructure for ultrasensitive detection of ochratoxin A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Nameghi, M.A., Danesh, N.M., Ramezani, M., Hassani, F.V., Abnous, K., Taghdisi, S.M., A fluorescent aptasensor based on a DNA pyramid nanostructure for ultrasensitive detection of ochratoxin A, Anal. Bioanal. Chem. 408 (2016) 5811-5818.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aptamer nanomedicine for cancer therapeutics: barriers and potential for translation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mohammadi, M., Salmasi, Z., Hashemi, M., Mosaffa, F., Abnous, K., Ramezani, M., Single-walled carbon nanotubes functionalized with aptamer and piperazine–polyethylenimine derivative for targeted siRNA delivery into breast cancer cells, Int. J. Pharm. 485 (2015) 50-60.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aggregation-induced emission luminogen-embedded silica nanoparticles containing DNA aptamers for targeted cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, X., Song, P., Peng, L., Tong, A., Xiang, Y., Aggregation-induced emission luminogen-embedded silica nanoparticles containing DNA aptamers for targeted cell imaging, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 8 (2015) 609-616.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in aptasensors based on graphene and graphene-like nanomaterials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ping, J., Zhou, Y., Wu, Y., Papper, V., Boujday, S., Marks, R.S., Steele, T.W., Recent advances in aptasensors based on graphene and graphene-like nanomaterials, Biosens. Bioelectron. 64 (2015) 373-385.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectroscopic study of the interaction of insulin and its aptamer–sensitive optical detection of insulin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Verdian-Doghaei, A., Housaindokht, M.R., Spectroscopic study of the interaction of insulin and its aptamer–sensitive optical detection of insulin, J. Lumin. 159 (2015) 1-8.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zheng, X.T., Ananthanarayanan, A., Luo, K.Q., Chen, P., Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications, Small 11 (2015) 1620-1636.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based fluorescent chemosensors for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and application of imaging in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jaafariasl, M., Shams, E., Amini, M.K., Silica gel modified carbon paste electrode for electrochemical detection of insulin, Electrochim. Acta 56 (2011) 4390.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Farbod Abazar received his M.Sc. degree from the University of Isfahan, Iran in 2018. His research has been mainly focused on fabrication and application of novel sensors and biosensors based on carbon nanomareials.

Abdollah Noorbakhsh is an Associate Professor at the Department of Nanotechnology Engineering, University of Isfahan, Isfahan, Iran. He received his M.Sc. degree on 2006 and his Ph.D. degree on 2010 from the University of Kurdistan, Sanandaj, Iran. His research interests are fabrication of electrochemical and optical biosensors.
